title: SEGMENT 12 — George Streisinger and the transparent vertebrate

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — George Streisinger and the transparent vertebrate
In 1981, a molecular biologist named George Streisinger at the University of Oregon published a short paper that, in retrospect, marks the founding of zebrafish biology.
The paper was titled "Production of clones of homozygous diploid zebra fish.
" It was technically modest.
But it represented Streisinger's bet — a deliberate decision, in the spirit of Brenner's worm bet sixteen years earlier — that the zebrafish was the right vertebrate for genetics.

Streisinger had spent his career in phage genetics — bacteriophages, the viruses that infect bacteria — and had been part of the heroic generation of molecular biologists who established the basic facts of gene structure and function.
But by the late 1970s, he wanted to do something harder.
He wanted to do vertebrate genetics.
Not bacterial, not yeast, not invertebrate — vertebrate, with the same body plan as humans.
And he wanted to do it at the scale and speed that the molecular geneticists were used to.
The challenge was: vertebrates are not, in general, friendly to genetics.
Mice take ten weeks per generation, produce a handful of pups per litter, and are expensive.
Frogs are big and slow.
Chicks are easy to embryo-manipulate but hard to breed for genetics.
What Streisinger wanted was a vertebrate that bred like a fly — fast, in huge numbers, in tanks you could keep on a shelf.
He picked the zebrafish, Danio rerio, a small striped tropical freshwater fish native to South Asia.
Aquarium hobbyists had been keeping them for decades.
Streisinger's lab adapted them for genetics, and the list of features he chose them for is striking.
One: they breed fast and abundantly.
A breeding pair lays about 200 eggs in a morning, every week.
A small colony of a few hundred fish can produce tens of thousands of embryos per week.
Two: they're cheap.
Tanks of water, dry food, a temperature-controlled room.
Compared to mice, zebrafish facilities cost a tenth as much per animal.
Three: they generate fast.
Generation time is about three months — slower than fly or worm, but fast for a vertebrate, far faster than mouse.
Four — and this is the killer feature — the embryos are transparent.
Zebrafish embryos are essentially clear blobs of cells, in a thin clear chorion, sitting in a dish of water.
You can put one under a microscope and watch every cell divide, migrate, and differentiate, in real time, without dissecting anything.
The vertebrate equivalent of the worm's transparency — and almost as informative.
Five: external fertilization.
Female fish lay eggs into water; males fertilize them externally.
You can collect the fertilized eggs immediately, manipulate them, inject them with DNA or RNA, and watch development proceed.
No surgery, no implantation, no womb.
The embryo is on a microscope slide.
Six: vertebrate body plan.
Notochord, neural tube, somites, fins where our limbs would be, two-chambered heart that pumps blood through a circulatory system — all the standard vertebrate features.
Most of what zebrafish do at the molecular level, mammals also do.
The analogy: the zebrafish is the transparent vertebrate — Streisinger's idea: you want a vertebrate, you want lots of embryos, you want to SEE them develop; zebrafish gives you all three.
The combination of fast breeding, external fertilization, and transparent embryos is unique.
No other vertebrate has all three.
And the body plan is conserved enough with humans that what you learn in fish often translates to clinically relevant biology.
Streisinger died in 1984, only three years after his founding paper, but his lab and his students carried the project forward.
By the mid-1990s, zebrafish was an established model organism, with stock centers, standardized protocols, and a growing community.
The big idea: Streisinger's choice of zebrafish was the second great organism choice of late-twentieth-century biology, after Brenner's choice of the worm.
Brenner gave us invertebrate genetics at single-cell resolution.
Streisinger gave us vertebrate genetics at embryo-watching scale.
Different niches, different organisms, both deliberately chosen.
